labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.2.1.1 Importance of water and electrolyte balance for body functioning
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The trigger and response relate directly to water and electrolyte balance after ingestion of concentrated salt solution.

options.markscheme: Aldosterone is primarily involved in the regulation of sodium and potassium levels; it is released in response to low blood pressure or low sodium, not high plasma osmolarity.

ADH (also known as vasopressin) is secreted by the posterior pituitary in response to high plasma osmolarity. It communicates with the kidneys to increase the insertion of aquaporins in the collecting ducts, increasing water reabsorption and reducing urine volume.
Somatotropin (growth hormone) is responsible for stimulating growth, cell reproduction, and cell regeneration, and does not play a direct role in acute osmoregulation.
Oxytocin is a hormone involved in social bonding, childbirth, and milk ejection; it is not the primary regulator of blood solute concentration.

questions.specification: When a sprinter hears the starting gun, which pathway is activated first?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A.1 Communication > A.1.1 Inter-system communication > A.1.1.1 The nervous system and physiological response coordination
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.1.1.1 The nervous system and physiological response coordination
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks which pathway is activated first when hearing the starting gun—this is a rapid neural (sensory receptor → CNS/spinal cord → skeletal muscle) response coordinated by the nervous system, not endocrine, respiratory, or cardiovascular transport.

options.content: Sensory receptors (ear) $\rightarrow$ sensory neurone $\rightarrow$ CNS (brain) $\rightarrow$ motor neurone $\rightarrow$ skeletal muscle
options.markscheme: Correct. The fastest initial response to hearing the starting gun is via the nervous system: auditory receptors detect the sound, impulses travel via sensory neurones to the CNS (brain for auditory processing), then motor neurones carry commands to skeletal muscle to initiate movement.
Incorrect. Hormonal responses are slower than neural responses because hormones must be transported in the bloodstream, so this is not the first pathway activated.
Incorrect. This is the gas exchange/respiratory pathway supporting aerobic metabolism, not the immediate signalling pathway for reacting to a sound.
Incorrect. The circulatory system supplies oxygen/nutrients, but it does not provide the rapid signalling that initiates movement; this is controlled by neural pathways.

questions.specification:
The graph below shows heart rate ($\text{bpm}$) and systolic blood pressure ($\text{mmHg}$) during a 20-minute moderate-intensity treadmill run.

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/nK5MFLrBU6K8SE1cKbt7O)

Which physiological mechanism best explains the plateau in blood pressure despite a continuing rise in heart rate?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A.1 Communication > A.1.2 Maintaining homeostasis > A.1.2.1 Mechanisms and processes maintaining homeostasis
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.1.2.1 Mechanisms and processes maintaining homeostasis
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks why systolic blood pressure plateaus during ongoing exercise, pointing to a homeostatic negative-feedback mechanism (baroreceptor reflex) regulating arterial pressure via peripheral vasodilation.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A.1 Communication > A.1.3 Transport > A.1.3.1 The cardiovascular system and bodily functions
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.1.3.1 The cardiovascular system and bodily functions
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Involves cardiovascular responses to exercise (heart rate and blood pressure changes) and mechanisms affecting cardiac output and total peripheral resistance.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A.1 Communication > A.1.1 Inter-system communication > A.1.1.1 The nervous system and physiological response coordination
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.1.1.1 The nervous system and physiological response coordination
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The mechanisms listed (parasympathetic/vagal tone, baroreceptor reflex, chemoreceptors) are nervous-system control pathways coordinating physiological responses.

options.content: Enhanced parasympathetic feedback limiting myocardial contraction
options.markscheme: Incorrect. Parasympathetic activity is significantly reduced (withdrawn) during moderate-intensity exercise to allow for increases in heart rate and contractility.

options.content: Local metabolic vasodilation in active muscles reducing total peripheral resistance
options.markscheme: Correct. During dynamic exercise, heart rate (and therefore cardiac output) can continue to rise, but arteriolar vasodilation in active skeletal muscle lowers total peripheral resistance. Since mean arterial pressure is approximately $\text{MAP} \approx \text{CO} \times \text{TPR}$, a fall in TPR can offset a rise in CO, producing a plateau in blood pressure.
Incorrect. Chemoreceptors stimulate an increase in cardiac output and ventilation in response to changes in blood chemistry (such as increased $\text{CO}_2$ or decreased pH) during exercise.

options.content: Increased vagal tone causing vasoconstriction in the periphery
options.markscheme: Incorrect. Vagal tone (parasympathetic) decreases during exercise, and the vagus nerve does not mediate peripheral vasoconstriction; vasoconstriction is a sympathetic nervous system response.
